name: web
contactName: Oliver Terbu, Mike Xu, Dmitri Zagidulin, Amy Guy

Decentralization.detail: Relies on DNS and web hosting infrastructure. Domain registrars and hosting providers are centralized intermediaries. However, the method itself has no single central authority.

Control.detail: Domain owner has full control over the DID Document hosted at their domain. They can update, rotate keys, or deactivate at will.

Privacy.detail: DID Documents are publicly accessible via HTTPS. The domain name itself may reveal the entity's identity. No built-in selective disclosure.

Security.detail: Relies on TLS/HTTPS for transport security. DID Document integrity depends on the web server's security posture.

Proof_Based.detail: DID Documents contain verification methods (public keys) that enable cryptographic proof of controller identity.

Persistence.detail: DIDs persist as long as the domain is maintained. Domain expiration or transfer breaks the identifier. Not as persistent as ledger-anchored methods.

Crypto_Verifiability.detail: Supports Ed25519, P-256, P-384, and other verification methods in the DID Document.

Crypto_Extensibility.detail: Any verification method type can be included in the DID Document. Not limited to a single algorithm.

Discoverability.detail: DIDs are discoverable via well-known URI pattern: https://<domain>/.well-known/did.json

Interoperability.detail: Widely implemented. Supported by Veramo, SpruceID, Walt.id, and most DID toolkits. Universal Resolver driver available.

Portability.detail: DIDs are tied to DNS domains. Changing domains means changing the DID. This is the primary limitation and the motivation for did:webvh.

Inter_Jurisdictional.detail: DNS is global, but domain registration is subject to national regulations (ccTLDs, ICANN policies). The DID itself works across jurisdictions.

Key_Rotation.detail: Update the DID Document on the web server to rotate keys. No on-chain transaction needed.

Uniqueness.detail: DNS guarantees domain uniqueness globally. One domain = one did:web identifier.

Service_Endpoints.detail: Full service endpoint support in the DID Document. Any service type can be declared.

Composability.detail: Composes with Verifiable Credentials, DIDComm, Well-Known DID Configuration, and other standards.

Equivalence.detail: Supports alsoKnownAs property to declare equivalence with other DIDs.

Legal_Compatibility.detail: Domain ownership provides a clear legal entity mapping. Compatible with eIDAS and other regulatory frameworks.

Metadata.detail: Basic resolution metadata supported. No built-in versioning metadata (addressed by did:webvh).

DID_Resolution.detail: Resolution via HTTPS GET to /.well-known/did.json or /<path>/did.json. Simple, cacheable, CDN-friendly.

CRUD_Operations.detail: Create: host DID Document. Read: HTTPS GET. Update: replace file. Deactivate: remove file or return 410 Gone.

Deactivation.detail: Remove the DID Document or return HTTP 410 Gone. No revocation list needed.
